title: HAVEN: A Value Exchange Protocol for Patient-Controlled Health Data
author: Qiande, Moni
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description:
This paper presents HAVEN (Health Asset Value & Exchange Network), an open protocol for patient-controlled health data governance.   Health data interoperability standards like FHIR R4 and OMOP CDM have matured rapidly, yet no corresponding governance layer   ensures that consent, audit trails, and value attribution travel with the data. Foundation models are being trained on clinical   records without patient consent mechanisms in place. 
   HAVEN specifies four composable primitives: (1) a content-addressed Health Asset that structurally requires a consent reference   before creation; (2) a Consent Protocol with closed-world semantics, deterministic verification, and immediate revocation; (3) a   hash-chained Provenance Record supporting O(log n) Merkle inclusion proofs; and (4) a quality-gated Contribution Model that   quantifies patient data value through a tier-weighted formula. The protocol layers over FHIR R4, OMOP CDM 6.0, and SMART on FHIR   without introducing new data formats. 
   We prove soundness and completeness of the consent verification algorithm, establish tamper-evidence of the provenance chain under   standard cryptographic assumptions (SHA-256, Ed25519), and characterize the computational complexity of all core operations.   Storage, encryption, key management, and payment mechanisms are intentionally left to implementers. 
   The protocol specification is released under CC BY 4.0. Formal specifications, TypeSpec definitions, and test vectors are available    at https://github.com/Chesterguan/HAVEN.
steps_raw:
This paper presents HAVEN (Health Asset Value & Exchange Network), an open protocol for patient-controlled health data governance.   Health data interoperability standards like FHIR R4 and OMOP CDM have matured rapidly, yet no corresponding governance layer   ensures that consent, audit trails, and value attribution travel with the data. Foundation models are being trained on clinical   records without patient consent mechanisms in place.

The protocol layers over FHIR R4, OMOP CDM 6.0, and SMART on FHIR   without introducing new data formats. 
   We prove soundness and completeness of the consent verification algorithm, establish tamper-evidence of the provenance chain under   standard cryptographic assumptions (SHA-256, Ed25519), and characterize the computational complexity of all core operations.   Storage, encryption, key management, and payment mechanisms are intentionally left to implementers. 
   The protocol specification is released under CC BY 4.0. Formal specifications, TypeSpec definitions, and test vectors are available    at https://github.com/Chesterguan/HAVEN.
